value: Intermittent painless gross hematuria for more than 3 months.

Three months ago, the patient first noticed that the entire stream of urine was red, similar to meat-washing water, with small clots. The discoloration was present from the beginning to the end of voiding and was not painful. Episodes occurred intermittently. He also had mild straining, a sensation of incomplete emptying, and nocturia 1–2 times/night. No flank pain, fever, abdominal pain, or weight loss was reported. He initially visited a local clinic but no systematic workup was completed.
Gross hematuria.
Total hematuria.
Meat-washing-water appearance.
Small clots are present.
Painless during urination.
Denies fever or chills.
Denies flank pain.
Mild straining, incomplete emptying, nocturia 1–2 times/night.
No foamy urine, edema, hypertension-related renal symptoms, or glomerular clues; urinalysis is dominated by marked hematuria.

Hypertension for 10 years, controlled with valsartan once daily. Occasional exertional precordial discomfort and chest tightness. Denies known diabetes, liver disease, tuberculosis, drug allergy, previous major surgery, or transfusion. Family generally healthy.
Long-term aspirin use; valsartan for hypertension.
Retired man, worried about cancer, communicates clearly. Initially emphasizes red urine and does not volunteer aspirin use or clots unless asked.
Vital signs stable. No obvious pallor. Abdomen soft and non-tender. No costovertebral-angle tenderness. No suprapubic distension. External genitalia unremarkable.
Urine RBC 5562/μL.
CT urography: space-occupying lesion on the left bladder wall. Ultrasound: multiple broad-based lesions on the left bladder wall, largest about 3 × 3 cm.
Malignant bladder tumor.
Older male with painless gross hematuria, clots, and antiplatelet use. Bladder cancer must be considered; hematuria must not be attributed solely to aspirin.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO blood group, coagulation tests, infectious screening, PSA panel, tumor markers, HbA1c.

Cardiopulmonary evaluation: ECG, chest CT, non-contrast cardiac CT + coronary CTA, echocardiography. Tumor evaluation: pelvic MRI with contrast and DWI for muscle invasion. Metastatic workup: bone scan.

Stop aspirin and bridge with low-molecular-weight heparin if indicated. Continue blood pressure control. Complete diagnostic and preoperative evaluation, exclude surgical contraindications, arrange bladder tumor biopsy/TURBT, then formulate surgical plan.
HbA1c 8.0%; syphilis antibody positive; chest CT shows a 1 cm left upper-lung nodule, AIS not excluded; coronary CTA shows LAD 30%, RCA 40%, LCX 40–50% stenosis.

Monitor capillary glucose fasting and postprandial; diabetic diet and glucose optimization. Complete syphilis confirmatory testing. Request cardiology/endocrinology evaluation as needed. Use pathology and staging to decide radical cystectomy and urinary diversion.
1. Bladder malignant tumor (T2N0M0). 2. Hypertension. 3. Coronary atherosclerotic heart disease. 4. Diabetes mellitus.

Bladder biopsy shows high-grade urothelial carcinoma. Bowel preparation before urinary diversion; glycemic target fasting 6–8 mmol/L and 2h postprandial 8–10 mmol/L. Stoma clinic for abdominal stoma marking. Switch to calcium-channel blocker on operation day as appropriate.
Laparoscopic radical cystectomy with Bricker ileal conduit urinary diversion.
Urology, radiology, pathology, cardiology, endocrinology, anesthesiology, stoma therapy clinic; oncology and thoracic surgery if needed.
Clarify muscle invasion and staging; assess coronary stenosis and aspirin interruption/bridging; optimize diabetes; evaluate lung nodule; plan radical cystectomy and urinary diversion safely.

Deduct points if hematuria phase, clots, painless nature, aspirin, smoking/occupational exposure are not asked. High-risk error if hematuria is attributed only to aspirin. Deduct if CTU, cystoscopy/TURBT pathology, or pelvic MRI staging is omitted.
Recurrent left flank pain with hematuria for more than 1 month.

One month ago the patient developed left flank aching pain without radiation. With changes in body position she noticed the entire urine stream became red like meat-washing water, with worm-like clots, from start to finish. Urination itself was not painful. The symptom recurred intermittently. No obvious frequency, urgency, or dysuria. No fever or chills.
Gross hematuria.
Total hematuria.
Meat-washing-water appearance.
Worm-like clots.
Left flank aching pain; no dysuria.
Denies fever or chills.
Left flank aching pain.
No obvious frequency, urgency, or dysuria.
No foamy urine, edema, or post-infectious tea-colored urine.

Rheumatic heart disease with mechanical valve replacement; long-term anticoagulation. Cerebral infarction 2 years ago, recovered after conservative treatment. Denies diabetes, liver disease, tuberculosis, smoking, or heavy drinking. Penicillin allergy.
Long-term hypnotic use (estazolam reported); warfarin anticoagulation due to mechanical valve.
Elderly woman with valve replacement, anxious about surgery and stopping anticoagulation. Medication history must be actively asked.
Vital signs stable. Mild left CVA tenderness possible. Abdomen soft. Irregular rhythm and valvular murmur. No lower limb edema.
Urine RBC 4768/μL; WBC 45/μL.
Ultrasound: left renal and ureteral hydronephrosis, possible mid-ureteral obstruction. CT: soft tissue lesion in the mid-left ureter, highly suspicious for malignancy.
Left ureteral malignant tumor.
Upper-tract mass with hydronephrosis. Warfarin does not explain hematuria by itself; malignancy workup is required.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO blood group, coagulation, infectious screening, tumor markers, HbA1c; urine cytology on 3 consecutive days.

Cardiopulmonary evaluation: ECG, chest CT, cardiac CT + coronary CTA, echocardiography. Brain MRI with DWI due to stroke history. CTU to define ureteral lesion. Renal scan for split renal function. Staging imaging as needed.
Stop warfarin and bridge with low-molecular-weight heparin under specialist guidance. Complete evaluation, confirm diagnosis, and exclude surgical contraindications.
Marked hyperlipidemia. ECG shows atrial fibrillation. Coronary CTA: LAD 30%, RCA 40%, LCX 40–50% stenosis; echocardiography may show valvular dysfunction and reduced cardiac reserve.

Low-salt, low-fat diet and statin therapy. Holter monitoring for atrial fibrillation. Urine cytology shows atypical urothelial cells; consider urine methylation test and definitive CTU/ureteroscopic pathology if needed.
1. Left ureteral malignant tumor (T2N0M0). 2. Hyperlipidemia. 3. Atrial fibrillation. 4. Coronary atherosclerosis.
Bowel preparation, blood preparation, ICU bed reservation if high risk, anticoagulation interruption on operation day with bridging strategy planned by cardiology/cardiac surgery/anesthesiology.
Laparoscopic left radical nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff excision.
Urology, radiology, cardiology, cardiac surgery, anesthesiology; hematology/transfusion medicine if needed.
Assess staging and surgical plan; determine perioperative management of warfarin in a mechanical-valve/atrial fibrillation patient; evaluate reduced EF and valvular regurgitation risk.

Deduct if mechanical valve, warfarin, or stroke history is not asked. Deduct if CTU, urine cytology, cystoscopy, or split renal function is omitted. Deduct if cardiology/cardiac surgery/anesthesia risk evaluation is not requested.
Recurrent hematuria for over 5 months, worsened for 1 week.

Five months ago he developed painless gross hematuria without obvious trigger. Urine was bright red with a small amount of clots and the whole stream was red. There was no dysuria, frequency, urgency, or obstructive symptoms. No flank pain or fever. Symptoms recurred intermittently and worsened in the last week.
Gross hematuria.
Total hematuria.
Bright red.
Small amount of clots.
Painless.
Denies fever.
No flank pain.
No frequency, urgency, or obstructive symptoms.
No foamy urine, edema, or glomerulonephritis clues.

COPD with cough and white foamy sputum. Hypertension for over 20 years, controlled with irbesartan. Denies diabetes, liver disease, tuberculosis. Smokes for over 30 years, about 1 pack/day; drinks small amounts. No known drug allergy.
Long-term aspirin and irbesartan.
Elderly long-term smoker with sputum production; worries he may not tolerate surgery. Smoking and COPD history must be actively asked.
COPD signs may include barrel-chest tendency and coarse breath sounds. Abdomen soft. No CVA tenderness. No obvious lower limb edema.
Urine RBC 4448/μL; WBC 3/μL.
Urinary CT suggests a soft tissue lesion in the right renal pelvis, about 2 × 3 cm.
Right renal pelvis malignant tumor.
70-year-old male, heavy smoking history, painless total gross hematuria and weight loss risk suggest high-risk urothelial cancer.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO blood group, coagulation, infectious screening, tumor markers, HbA1c, arterial blood gas; urine cytology for 3 consecutive days.

ECG, chest CT, cardiac CT + coronary CTA, echocardiography, pulmonary function tests. CTU for right renal pelvic lesion. Renal scan for split function. Staging imaging including chest/abdominal assessment.

Airway preparation with nebulization and sputum management. Smoking cessation. Continue blood pressure control. Stop aspirin if primary prevention without bridging. Complete evaluation and exclude surgical contraindications.
ABG shows PaO2 about 85 mmHg. Chest CT shows multiple pulmonary bullae. Coronary CTA critical value: LAD stenosis >75%, RCA 40%, LCX 40–50%.
Nebulization, bronchodilation, sputum clearance, low-flow oxygen and pulse oximetry. Cardiology consult for CAG/stent and operative risk. Respiratory consult for perioperative pulmonary management.
1. Right renal pelvis malignant tumor (T2N0M0). 2. COPD. 3. Pulmonary bullae. 4. Coronary atherosclerotic heart disease.
Bowel preparation, blood preparation, ICU bed reservation. Use LMWH if indicated; oral atorvastatin for plaque stabilization. Optimize airway and coronary risk before major surgery.
Cardiology may require CAG and coronary stenting first; oncology consult to evaluate neoadjuvant therapy. Elective laparoscopic right nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff excision after optimization.
Urology, radiology, cardiology, respiratory medicine, anesthesiology, ICU, medical oncology; thoracic surgery if needed.
Evaluate staging and timing of nephroureterectomy; determine whether LAD >75% stenosis needs CAG/stent; optimize COPD and airway management; decide if neoadjuvant therapy should be given first.

Deduct if smoking, COPD symptoms, aspirin, or weight loss are not asked. Deduct if severe coronary stenosis is not recognized and consulted. Deduct if CTU, urine cytology, renal scan, or chest imaging is omitted.
Painless total gross hematuria for more than 1 month.

Over one month ago he developed painless gross hematuria. Urine was bright red and the entire stream was red. No frequency, urgency, dysuria, flank pain, or fever. He did not pay attention initially. One week ago symptoms worsened and a local CT suggested a huge right renal mass, possible vena cava tumor thrombus, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and destruction of the left second rib with a soft tissue mass. He then came to the hospital.
Gross hematuria.
Total hematuria.
Bright red.
No obvious clots noticed unless specifically asked.
Painless.
Denies fever.
No flank pain.
No frequency, urgency, or obstructive symptoms.
No glomerular clues.

Hypertension for over 20 years, controlled with benazepril, nifedipine sustained-release tablets and hydrochlorothiazide. Type 2 diabetes treated with insulin, dapagliflozin and metformin; glucose monitoring unclear. Denies heart disease, liver disease, tuberculosis, major surgery, or transfusion unless otherwise specified.
Benazepril, nifedipine SR, hydrochlorothiazide, dapagliflozin, metformin, and insulin.
Elderly male, anxious about tumor thrombus and metastasis. Diabetes and hypertension medications must be actively asked.
May have mild pallor. Right upper abdomen/flank fullness may be palpable. CVA tenderness not prominent. Lower limb edema should be checked.
Urine RBC 4448/μL; WBC 3/μL.
Urinary CT: huge right renal mass, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, destruction of left second rib with soft tissue formation; possible vena cava involvement needs further MRI/vascular evaluation.
Right renal malignant tumor.
Huge renal mass with suspected vena cava tumor thrombus and bone metastasis; complex oncologic MDT case.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO blood group, coagulation, infectious screening, tumor markers, HbA1c, PSA panel; urine cytology for 3 consecutive days.

ECG, enhanced chest CT, cardiac CT + coronary CTA, echocardiography. CTU for renal tumor. MRI/vascular ultrasound for tumor thrombus. Bone scan for skeletal metastasis. Whole-body staging imaging as needed.

Monitor glucose fasting and postprandial; diabetic diet and optimize glycemia. Continue blood pressure control. Complete staging and operative-risk evaluation; organize MDT before deciding surgery/systemic therapy.

HbA1c 7.6%. Enhanced chest CT: multiple inflammatory lung nodules; left second rib destruction with soft-tissue mass, metastasis considered. Bone scan: uptake in left second rib and T2 vertebra. Imaging suggests advanced renal tumor with thrombus/metastasis.

Optimize glucose to fasting 6–8 mmol/L and postprandial 8–10 mmol/L. MDT: thoracic surgery for rib metastasis resection possibility, spine surgery for vertebral lesion, vascular surgery for tumor thrombus, oncology for systemic therapy, anesthesia/ICU for operative risk.
Right renal malignant tumor with suspected venous tumor thrombus and bone metastasis; hypertension; type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Major surgery only after staging and MDT. Assess thrombus level, cardiopulmonary reserve, bleeding risk, transfusion needs, ICU bed, glucose/BP control and VTE prevention.
MDT-guided plan: consider cytoreductive nephrectomy/thrombectomy only if appropriate; otherwise systemic therapy, metastasis-directed therapy, or staged surgery according to risk and resectability.
Urology, radiology, vascular surgery, anesthesiology, ICU, medical oncology, thoracic surgery, spine/orthopedics, transfusion medicine.
Define thrombus level, metastatic burden, operability, need for nephrectomy/thrombectomy, systemic therapy sequence, and perioperative safety plan.
Deduct if large renal mass with thrombus/metastasis does not trigger MDT; deduct if staging, venous imaging, bone scan, glucose control, vascular/anesthesia/ICU consults are omitted.
Progressive dysuria/voiding difficulty for over 3 years, worsened with hematuria for 1 month.

Three years ago he developed frequency and urgency. If unable to void promptly, he could wet his pants. He also had straining, weak stream, split stream, occasional blood in urine and nocturia. Symptoms worsened progressively; nocturia increased to 3–4 and later 5–6 times/night, with recurrent hematuria and obstructive symptoms.
Gross hematuria mainly; microscopic RBC elevated.
Intermittent hematuria, partially relieved by increased water intake; ask whether terminal/total.
Bright red.
No obvious clots if asked.
Dysuria and suprapubic distension when obstruction worsens.
Denies fever.
Denies back or limb pain.
Frequency, urgency, weak and split stream, nocturia 5–6 times/night, urge incontinence, voiding difficulty.
No foamy urine, edema, or post-infectious hematuria.

Diabetes for over 10 years treated with dapagliflozin and metformin, glucose monitoring unclear. Coronary stent implantation 6 years ago. Denies liver disease and tuberculosis. Allergic to sulfonamides and Qingkailing. Denies transfusion. Family generally healthy.
Long-term aspirin, clopidogrel, dapagliflozin and metformin.
Elderly male most concerned about being unable to void. Hesitant about prostate biopsy; coronary stent and dual antiplatelet therapy require active questioning.
Possible suprapubic bladder distension. Digital rectal exam should assess enlarged, hard, nodular prostate and median sulcus. External genitalia unremarkable.
Urine RBC 328/μL; WBC 88/μL.
Ultrasound: bladder trabeculation, enlarged prostate 56 × 65 × 47 mm, heterogeneous internal echo.
Benign prostatic hyperplasia initially, but prostate cancer must be ruled out.
Elderly male with markedly elevated PSA, PI-RADS 5 lesion and possible seminal vesicle invasion. Prostate cancer must be considered; dual antiplatelet therapy does not explain hematuria alone.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO, coagulation, infectious screening, tumor markers, PSA panel, HbA1c.
ECG, chest CT, cardiac CT + coronary CTA, echocardiography. Pelvic MRI with contrast and DWI for prostate assessment. Urodynamic study for lower urinary tract function.

Monitor fasting and postprandial glucose. Diabetic diet; switch oral hypoglycemics to insulin if needed. Assess antiplatelet therapy interruption with cardiology. Complete evaluation and plan biopsy/surgery.
tPSA 32 ng/mL, fPSA 1.2 ng/mL. Coronary CTA critical: LAD stenosis >70%, RCA 30%, LCX 40–50%. Pelvic MRI likely PI-RADS 5 with possible seminal vesicle involvement.

High suspicion of prostate cancer: proceed to prostate biopsy after antiplatelet and cardiac risk assessment. Consult cardiology for coronary stenosis/antiplatelet management; endocrinology for glucose control.
Suspected/confirmed prostate cancer with BPH/LUTS; coronary artery disease after stent; type 2 diabetes mellitus.
Assess dual antiplatelet interruption, bleeding/thrombosis risk, glucose control, infection control, anesthesia and cardiac risk before biopsy or surgery.
Transperineal or transrectal prostate biopsy for pathology; treatment depends on risk stage: active surveillance, radiotherapy, endocrine therapy, surgery or systemic therapy as appropriate.
Urology, cardiology, endocrinology, anesthesiology, radiology, pathology, oncology/radiotherapy as needed.
Clarify prostate cancer likelihood and staging, biopsy safety under dual antiplatelets, cardiac risk, and glucose optimization.
Deduct if PSA, DRE, prostate MRI, biopsy pathway, antiplatelet management or diabetes optimization are omitted; high-risk error if hematuria is attributed only to BPH or antiplatelets.
Frequency, urgency and dysuria with hematuria for 2 days.

Two days ago she suddenly developed stinging pain, incomplete emptying, and burning during urination. Frequency was marked, about every 10 minutes. After voiding, tissue paper showed pink urine. No clots, flank pain, or fever. She bought Sanjin tablets from a pharmacy herself and symptoms improved incompletely.
Light gross/pink urine; microscopic RBC elevated.
Not classic total hematuria; mainly pink staining after voiding. Ask to distinguish urethral urine from vaginal bleeding.
Pink.
No clots.
Stinging and burning with urination.
No fever or chills.
No flank pain.
Marked frequency, urgency, dysuria and incomplete emptying.
No foamy urine, edema, hypertension or post-infectious tea-colored urine.

Denies hypertension, diabetes, heart disease, liver disease and tuberculosis. No drug allergy, trauma or surgery. Unmarried and never pregnant. Menarche at 14; last menstrual period on the 15th of last month; regular 28–30 day cycle.
No regular medication.
Young woman irritated by pain and frequency. Somewhat embarrassed about gynecologic/menstrual questions; requires gentle questioning.
Afebrile. Mild suprapubic tenderness. No CVA tenderness. Exclude gynecologic bleeding if indicated.
Urine RBC 48/μL; WBC 58/μL.
Urinary ultrasound unremarkable.
Urinary tract infection / acute cystitis.
Young woman; rule out menstrual/vaginal contamination, pregnancy and sexual activity-related infection. No high-risk tumor features.

Urinalysis with sediment. Midstream urine culture and susceptibility if symptoms are severe, recurrent, treatment failure occurs, or before antibiotics. CBC/CRP as needed. Renal function as needed. Pregnancy test if applicable.

Ultrasound already normal. CT is usually unnecessary for uncomplicated cystitis; consider ultrasound or CT KUB if fever, flank pain, recurrent infection, treatment failure, or suspected stone/obstruction.
Empirical oral antibiotics, hydration, avoid irritant foods, symptomatic analgesia if needed, and education to complete the course.
RBC and WBC elevated in urine; nitrite may be positive or negative. Culture may grow E. coli. No fever and no CVA tenderness.
Adjust antibiotics according to culture. Recheck urinalysis in about 1 week. If hematuria persists or recurrent gross hematuria occurs, enter formal hematuria pathway to exclude stones/tumor.
Acute uncomplicated cystitis with hematuria; rule out menstrual/vaginal contamination.
Non-surgical case; focus on infection control, pregnancy/gynecologic exclusion if needed, antimicrobial stewardship and follow-up.
Short-course oral antibiotics guided by local resistance/culture; hydration and follow-up urinalysis.
Usually no MDT. Gynecology if vaginal bleeding/contamination suspected; infectious disease if recurrent/resistant/complicated infection.
Clarify source of bleeding, pregnancy/gynecologic contamination, need for culture-directed therapy and further workup if hematuria persists.
Deduct if frequency/urgency/dysuria, fever/flank pain, menstrual contamination, pregnancy, culture indication or follow-up urinalysis are not addressed.
Recurrent voiding difficulty for over 5 years, acute worsening with hematuria for 1 day.

Five years ago he developed split stream, weak stream, marked frequency about every half hour, and nocturia 4–5 times/night. A local hospital considered prostatic enlargement. He took prostate medication intermittently. Recently he developed acute urinary retention and light-red urine after catheterization.
Light gross hematuria plus microscopic hematuria.
Light red urine after catheterization/retention; may have terminal hematuria. Must differentiate from tumor-related hematuria.
Light red.
No clots reported.
Voiding difficulty with dysuria and lower abdominal distension.
No fever.
No back/flank pain.
Weak stream, split stream, frequency, nocturia 4–5 times/night, acute urinary retention.
No foamy urine, edema, or post-infectious hematuria.
Denies hypertension, diabetes, liver disease and tuberculosis. No known drug allergy. Non-smoker. Drinks 50–100 mL liquor/day. Denies transfusion. Children healthy.
No regular medication recorded; answer as none if asked.
Elderly male most anxious about inability to urinate; has concerns about DRE and surgery.
Suprapubic bladder distension/tenderness before catheterization, improved afterward. DRE: enlarged firm prostate with shallow median sulcus. No CVA tenderness.
Urine RBC 78/μL; WBC 88/μL; tPSA 7 ng/mL, fPSA 0.5 ng/mL.
Ultrasound: thickened bladder wall, catheter balloon in bladder, enlarged prostate 56 × 48 mm protruding into bladder.
Benign prostatic hyperplasia.
Older male with enlarged prostate and PSA 7; prostate cancer must be excluded. Alcohol may precipitate retention.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO, coagulation, infectious screening, tumor markers, HbA1c.
ECG, chest CT and echocardiography. Pelvic MRI with contrast/DWI for prostate. Urodynamics for lower urinary tract function.
Treat UTI with oral antibiotics; use tamsulosin and finasteride to improve voiding. Complete diagnostic evaluation and cardiopulmonary assessment; plan surgery if indicated.
Urodynamics: severe bladder outlet obstruction and weakened detrusor contractility. Pelvic MRI: enlarged prostate with multiple nodules, PI-RADS 3, protruding into bladder.
Consult as needed; exclude prostate cancer if clinically indicated; consider TURP/laser enucleation based on obstruction, retention, bladder function and surgical risk.
BPH with bladder outlet obstruction, acute urinary retention, UTI/hematuria; elevated PSA requiring prostate cancer exclusion.
Treat infection, evaluate residual urine and bladder function, assess anesthesia/cardiopulmonary risk, control bleeding risk, and counsel on urinary retention/recurrence.
Medical therapy or surgical treatment such as TURP/laser enucleation after evaluation.
Urology primarily; anesthesiology and cardiology if operative risk; pathology if biopsy/resection specimen abnormal.
Clarify whether PSA/MRI requires biopsy, assess surgical indication, retention management and perioperative risk.
Deduct if LUTS history, acute retention, DRE, PSA/MRI, infection evaluation or tumor exclusion are missed.
Intermittent hematuria with interrupted urinary stream for more than 1 year.

For one year he has had intermittent gross hematuria, mainly light red urine at the end of urination. He has mild stinging during urination. Sometimes the urinary stream suddenly stops; after changing body position he can void again. No clots, flank pain, or fever. Nocturia 1–2 times/night.
Gross hematuria.
Predominantly terminal hematuria.
Light red.
No clots.
Mild stinging during urination; position-related interruption of stream.
No fever.
No flank pain.
Interrupted stream that improves after postural change; nocturia 1–2 times/night.
No glomerular clues.

HBV carrier/small three positives, untreated. Denies hypertension, diabetes, and tuberculosis. No known drug allergy. Smokes for over 20 years, half a pack/day. Occasional social drinking. Denies transfusion. Children healthy.
Long-term aspirin.
Middle-aged man troubled by intermittent stream interruption. Does not volunteer smoking or aspirin unless asked.
Usually no significant suprapubic tenderness. No CVA tenderness. DRE may show enlarged prostate. Bladder percussion depends on residual urine.
Urine RBC 279/μL; WBC 3/μL.
Ultrasound: enlarged prostate 52 × 41 mm; mobile hyperechoic lesion in bladder with postural change, consistent with bladder stone.
Bladder stone with benign prostatic hyperplasia.
Smoking history and aspirin use; still must exclude bladder tumor, although stone+BPH strongly explains symptoms.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO, coagulation, infectious screening, PSA panel, tumor markers, HbA1c.
ECG, chest CT, echocardiography. Pelvic CT to assess prostate and stones. Urodynamics for lower urinary tract function.
Tamsulosin and finasteride for LUTS. Complete evaluation and surgical planning.
Urodynamics: moderate bladder outlet obstruction, normal detrusor contractility, residual urine 60 mL. Pelvic CT: prostate 54 × 43 × 42 mm; multiple bladder stones 1–2 cm.
Plan cystolithotripsy/cystolitholapaxy and address bladder outlet obstruction if indicated; continue tumor exclusion if hematuria features are atypical or persistent.
Bladder stones; BPH with bladder outlet obstruction; hematuria.
Evaluate infection, coagulation/aspirin, prostate obstruction, anesthesia risk and recurrence prevention.
Endoscopic bladder stone removal; consider simultaneous or staged BPH surgery depending on obstruction.
Urology primarily; anesthesiology/cardiology if antiplatelet or surgical risk; infectious disease if UTI.
Clarify stone management, need to treat BPH, antiplatelet perioperative strategy, and whether cystoscopy is needed to exclude bladder tumor.
Deduct if terminal hematuria, interrupted stream/postural relief, stone imaging, BPH, smoking/aspirin and tumor exclusion are not assessed.
Sudden right flank pain with hematuria for half a day.

At midnight last night she developed sudden right flank cutting pain, paroxysmal and severe, radiating to the right lower abdomen and perineum, with nausea and vomiting. She had frequency and urgency. Urine became dark tea-colored. No fever. Stool normal.
Tea-colored gross hematuria / marked microscopic hematuria.
Hematuria appeared with renal colic; not typical initial/terminal; usually whole-urine discoloration.
Dark tea-colored.
No obvious clots.
Severe paroxysmal right renal colic radiating to right lower abdomen/perineum, with nausea/vomiting.
No fever.
Marked right renal colic.
Frequency and urgency.
No foamy urine or edema; tea-colored urine should be distinguished by sediment, but stone evidence is strong.

Denies diabetes, heart disease, liver disease, tuberculosis and previous stones. No smoking or alcohol. No drug allergy or transfusion. Unmarried and never pregnant; menarche at 14; last menstrual period on the 6th of last month, regular cycle.
No regular medication.
Young woman in severe pain and anxious; may answer impatiently. Menstrual history should be asked to rule out contamination.
Right CVA tenderness positive; possible right ureteral tract tenderness; no peritoneal irritation; vital signs stable.
Urine RBC 5689/μL; WBC 45/μL.
Ultrasound: multiple right renal stones; mild right hydronephrosis and ureteral dilatation; 9 × 8 mm stone in upper ureter; bladder unremarkable.
Right renal/ureteral stone with hydronephrosis and infection.
Upper ureteral stone 9 × 8 mm with mild hydronephrosis and pyuria; watch for progression to infected obstruction.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO, coagulation, infectious screening, urine culture.
ECG and chest X-ray. Non-contrast CT KUB/renal+pelvic CT for stone evaluation; KUB X-ray as appropriate.
Oral/IV antibiotics if infection suspected; antispasmodic and analgesic treatment such as drotaverine; complete imaging and prepare definitive stone plan.
CT shows multiple right renal stones, largest 10 × 12 mm, and upper ureteral stone 9 × 8 mm.

If no systemic infection: consider ureteroscopic lithotripsy or stent based on stone size/location. If fever, sepsis, AKI or worsening obstruction appears: urgent drainage first, stone treatment later.
Right upper ureteral stone with hydronephrosis; right renal stones; hematuria; mild urinary infection.
Confirm infection control, renal function, coagulation, pregnancy status, imaging, analgesia and drainage plan.
Ureteroscopic lithotripsy/stent placement; treat renal stones in staged fashion if needed.
Urology primarily; infectious disease if systemic infection/resistant bacteria; interventional radiology if nephrostomy needed; anesthesia if surgery.
Determine urgent drainage versus elective lithotripsy, infection control, and surgical timing.
Deduct if renal colic radiation, nausea/vomiting, fever, urine culture, CT KUB, pregnancy/menstruation, and infected obstruction red flags are not assessed.
Recurrent left flank dull pain with exercise-related hematuria for over 3 years, recurrence for 1 day.

Three years ago he developed left flank dull pain after fatigue, without radiation or urinary discomfort. After strenuous exercise he intermittently developed dark tea-colored urine. Yesterday after badminton he again had soy-sauce/dark urine with mild left flank discomfort. No frequency, urgency, dysuria or fever.
Gross hematuria / marked microscopic hematuria.
Whole-urine darkening after exercise; not typical terminal hematuria.
Soy-sauce or dark tea-colored.
No clots reported.
Mild left flank dull pain, not typical renal colic.
No fever.
Left flank dull pain.
No frequency, urgency, or dysuria.
Tea/soy color requires distinction from glomerular hematuria, but imaging shows stone clearly.
Denies diabetes, heart disease, liver disease, tuberculosis, and previous stones. No smoking or alcohol. No drug allergy or transfusion. Parents, spouse and children healthy.
No regular medication.
Middle-aged man who thinks dark urine after exercise may not be serious; clinicians must ask about pain, fever and stone history.
Mild left CVA tenderness. Abdomen soft. Afebrile. No lower limb edema.
Urine RBC 5689/μL; WBC 55/μL.
Ultrasound: mild renal pelvis dilation; left renal stone about 35 × 25 mm; bladder and prostate unremarkable.
Left staghorn renal calculus.
Left staghorn renal calculus about 35 × 30 mm with mild hydronephrosis and infection risk; requires urine culture and preoperative infection control.
CBC, urinalysis with sediment, stool routine, full biochemistry, ABO, coagulation, infectious screening, urine culture.
ECG, chest CT. CTU for upper urinary tract stone anatomy; KUB X-ray.
Antibiotic treatment if infection suspected; antispasmodic/analgesic for flank discomfort; complete CTU/KUB and plan definitive stone treatment.
CTU shows left staghorn calculus, range about 35 × 30 mm.
Control infection and assess renal anatomy/function; plan PCNL or staged stone surgery; culture-directed antibiotics if bacteriuria/pyuria.
Left staghorn renal calculus with hematuria; mild hydronephrosis/UTI risk.
Preoperative urine culture, infection control, renal function, coagulation, blood preparation, imaging road map for PCNL, and sepsis prevention.
Percutaneous nephrolithotomy (PCNL), staged if necessary; metabolic evaluation and recurrence prevention afterward.
Urology, anesthesiology, infectious disease if infection; interventional radiology if access/drainage issues.
Assess PCNL plan, infection control, bleeding risk and need for staged treatment.
Deduct if exercise association, flank pain, fever, urine culture, CTU/KUB, infection control and PCNL preparation are omitted.
Sore throat for 3 weeks and hematuria for 1 week.

Three weeks ago she developed tonsillitis with fever up to 38°C and bilateral lumbar aching. No cough or sputum. Eyelid edema was obvious in the morning. One week ago she again had tonsillitis with fever and then developed gross hematuria a few days after infection, light red or meat-washing-water in appearance. No frequency, urgency or dysuria. Appetite fair, no significant weight loss.
Gross hematuria with glomerular microscopic evidence.
Post-infectious hematuria; not localized by initial/terminal pattern; may appear as total light-red/meat-washing urine.
Light red or meat-washing-water appearance.
No clots.
No dysuria; bilateral lumbar aching may be present.
Sore throat/tonsillitis with fever up to 38°C.
Bilateral lumbar aching, not classic renal colic.
No frequency, urgency, or dysuria.
Eyelid edema, proteinuria, pathological casts, markedly dysmorphic RBCs, and post-infectious hematuria strongly suggest glomerular origin.
Denies hypertension, diabetes, liver disease, and tuberculosis. No known drug allergy. Denies transfusion. Vaccinations on schedule.
No regular medication recorded; answer as none if asked.
Adolescent girl; family anxious; expression is average. Sore throat and eyelid edema must be actively asked.
Blood pressure must be measured and may be elevated. Eyelid edema. Pharynx/tonsils congested. Check lower limb edema if needed. CVA tenderness not typical.
Urine RBC 279/μL; WBC 3/μL; protein positive; pathological casts positive; RBC phase: dysmorphic RBCs 9000.
No clear urologic imaging positive finding before admission. Renal ultrasound recommended to exclude structural disease.
Acute glomerulonephritis.
Adolescent post-infectious hematuria should prioritize glomerular origin, not tumor/stone pathway.

Urinalysis with sediment; dysmorphic RBC assessment; UPCR or 24-hour urine protein; renal function/eGFR; electrolytes; albumin; C3/C4; ASO; IgA; ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM as needed; HBV/HCV/HIV screening as needed.
Renal ultrasound for kidney size/structure. CTU is not first-line. If proteinuria persists, renal function worsens, or diagnosis is unclear, nephrology evaluates renal biopsy indication.

Nephrology consult. Rest, salt restriction, monitoring of blood pressure, urine output, weight and renal function. Treat infection focus. Use diuretics/antihypertensives as needed for edema/hypertension.
Protein positive, pathological casts positive, dysmorphic RBCs 9000; possible low C3 and elevated ASO; renal function normal or mildly abnormal; renal ultrasound usually without urologic mass.

Nephrology-led management. Serial urinalysis, proteinuria, creatinine and complement. If renal function worsens, proteinuria is marked, or diagnosis unclear, evaluate renal biopsy. Avoid unnecessary cystoscopy/CTU.
Acute glomerulonephritis / infection-related glomerulonephritis; proteinuria; eyelid edema.
Usually non-surgical. Focus on blood pressure, urine output, renal function, infection control and avoiding nephrotoxic drugs.
Nephrology follow-up and supportive therapy; BP control, salt restriction, diuresis if needed; immunologic treatment only according to nephrology assessment.
Nephrology mandatory; pediatric nephrology by age; infectious disease and rheumatology as needed.
Confirm glomerular hematuria, assess severity of acute nephritis, need for renal biopsy, and supportive/immunologic treatment strategy.

Deduct if sore throat/URI, edema, foamy urine, BP, proteinuria are not asked. Deduct if RBC morphology, protein quantification, complement, ASO, renal function are not ordered. High-risk error if nephrology safety net is not triggered.
Recurrent hematuria with increased foamy urine for over 6 months, worsened for half a month.

For half a year, after colds, he developed hematuria and increased foamy urine. 24-hour urine protein was 2.4 g. He took Huangkui capsules without relief. Half a month ago after staying up late, symptoms recurred and worsened; repeat 24-hour urine protein was 4.7 g with normal renal function. He was admitted for evaluation of proteinuria.
Gross/microscopic hematuria with marked proteinuria.
Recurrent post-URI hematuria; not initial/terminal; usually total urine color change.
May be tea-colored, light red, or gross hematuria, with foamy urine.
No clots.
No dysuria or renal colic.
Related to colds/upper respiratory infections; no persistent fever.
No typical flank pain.
No frequency, urgency, or dysuria.
Foamy urine, protein 3+, 24h protein increasing from 2.4 g to 4.7 g, dysmorphic RBCs 27000 strongly suggest glomerular origin.
Previously healthy.
No regular medication.
Young man worried about recurrent hematuria and foamy urine damaging kidney function; can clearly describe worsening after colds.
Check blood pressure and eyelid/lower limb edema. Abdominal and urologic exam often unremarkable. No renal colic signs.
Urinalysis: protein 3+, occult blood 3+, RBC 5689/μL; RBC morphology: dysmorphic RBCs 27000.
No clear urologic imaging positive finding before admission. Renal ultrasound recommended to exclude structural abnormality.
IgA nephropathy.
Young male with recurrent post-URI hematuria and proteinuria; IgA nephropathy should be considered. Do not treat as isolated stone/tumor pathway.

Urinalysis with sediment; RBC morphology; UPCR/ACR; 24-hour urine protein; renal function/eGFR; electrolytes; albumin; IgA; C3/C4; ANA, anti-dsDNA, ANCA, anti-GBM; HBV/HCV/HIV screening; coagulation and biopsy workup if needed.
Renal ultrasound for kidney size, cortex-medulla and structure. Nephrology evaluates renal biopsy. CTU/cystoscopy is not first-line.

Nephrology consult. Monitor BP, urine output, renal function and proteinuria. Low-salt diet; avoid nephrotoxic drugs. Consider RAS blockade/supportive therapy according to proteinuria and BP. Evaluate renal biopsy.
Protein 3+, occult blood 3+, dysmorphic RBCs 27000; 24-hour urine protein 4.7 g; renal function currently normal. Renal biopsy can confirm IgA nephropathy and Oxford classification.
Nephrology completes renal biopsy and classifies pathology/activity. Treatment depends on proteinuria, eGFR, BP and biopsy findings; regular monitoring of urine protein and renal function.
IgA nephropathy; proteinuria; glomerular hematuria.
Non-urologic surgical pathway. Before renal biopsy assess coagulation, BP, infection risk and informed consent. Avoid NSAIDs and other nephrotoxic drugs.
Nephrology follow-up. Proteinuria and BP control, renal protection, and steroid/immunosuppressive therapy only according to biopsy and guideline indications.
Nephrology mandatory; rheumatology if immune abnormalities; pathology for renal biopsy interpretation.
Clarify IgA nephropathy diagnosis, renal biopsy necessity, proteinuria target, immunotherapy indication and follow-up plan.

Deduct if foamy urine, URI trigger, proteinuria, BP and edema are not asked. Deduct if UPCR/24h protein, RBC morphology, immune tests or renal biopsy evaluation are omitted. High-risk error if nephrology is not consulted.
